items.q: Is the DBMS the same thing as the database?
items.correct: No - the database is the organised collection of data; the DBMS is the software layer that manages access to it, like MySQL or Oracle.
items.misconception: Yes - DBMS is simply the technical name for the data itself.
items.random_wrong: Yes - deleting the DBMS deletes all the data automatically.
items.why: Software and data blur because users meet them together. One DBMS can manage many databases - clear evidence the two are distinct things.

items.q: Can you safely delete a customer record while orders still reference it?
items.correct: No - referential integrity forbids leaving orphaned foreign keys; the DBMS will block the deletion or cascade it according to defined rules.
items.misconception: Yes - deleting a parent record has no effect on other tables.
items.random_wrong: Yes - the orders will automatically find a different customer.
items.why: Tables look independent, so cross-table effects surprise students. Foreign keys create dependencies, and integrity rules exist exactly to police them.

items.q: Is data redundancy good because it acts as a backup?
items.correct: No - uncontrolled duplication inside a database causes inconsistencies when copies diverge; backups are deliberate, separate, managed copies of the whole database.
items.misconception: Yes - storing each fact several times protects against data loss.
items.random_wrong: Yes - redundancy is required by data protection law.
items.why: Redundancy sounds protective. Within the live schema it is a defect normalisation removes; resilience is achieved by proper backup procedures instead.

items.correct: Software that controls storage, retrieval, security and access to a database
items.why: It's Software that controls storage, retrieval, security and access to a database. “The physical server room” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Every foreign key value matches an existing primary key in the referenced table
items.misconception: All tables have the same number of rows
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Every foreign key value matches an existing primary key in the referenced table. “All tables have the same number of rows” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The atomicity property of a transaction means:
items.correct: The transaction either completes entirely or has no effect at all
items.misconception: Only one user may ever use the database
items.random_wrong: Each transaction must involve a single table
items.why: It's The transaction either completes entirely or has no effect at all. “Only one user may ever use the database” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Uncontrolled data redundancy is a problem because it:
items.correct: Wastes storage and risks inconsistent copies of the same fact
items.why: It's Wastes storage and risks inconsistent copies of the same fact. “Makes the database completely unreadable” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In a one-to-many relationship between two tables, the foreign key is placed in how many of the tables?
items.why: Exact answer: 1 table. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
